texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Facile tailoring of the two-dimensional graphene oxide channels for gas separation †
texts.text: Facile tailoring of the two-dimensional graphene oxide channels for gas separation †

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jie Shen, Mengchen Zhang, Gongping Liu and Wanqin Jin*
texts.text: Jie Shen, Mengchen Zhang, Gongping Liu and Wanqin Jin*

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This work demonstrates a facile method of fi nely tailoring the graphene oxide (GO) nanochannels by controlling their surface oxygenated functionalities. The as-prepared membrane with subnanometer interlayer nanochannels (0.36 nm) and CO2-philic properties showed excellent preferential CO2 permeation performance, which o ff ers promising potential for CO2 capture.
texts.text: This work demonstrates a facile method of fi nely tailoring the graphene oxide (GO) nanochannels by controlling their surface oxygenated functionalities. The as-prepared membrane with subnanometer interlayer nanochannels (0.36 nm) and CO2-philic properties showed excellent preferential CO2 permeation performance, which o ff ers promising potential for CO2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two dimensional graphene-based materials with unique atomic thickness and excellent mass transport properties are gaining heightened attention for the development of a new family of separation membranes. 1 As a derivative of graphene, graphene oxide (GO) is now considered as a promising nano-building block for membranes, owing to its tunable microstructures and physiochemical properties as well as large-scale fabrication using a solution-based strategy. 2 Through facile oxidization and exfoliation of bulk graphite, a large number of oxygenated functionalities can be chemically bonded on both basal planes and sheets edges of carbon layers, 3 endowing the two dimensional (2D) GO nanosheets with rich chemistry property. As a result, it has been well demonstrated 1,4 -7 that GO-based membranes with well-assembled laminar structure can be engineered to provide nanochannels for selective molecular transport. This leads to a success application of GO-based membranes in water treatment due to the highly hydrophilic characteristic. 4 -10 Generally, separation properties of membrane can be  nely tuned by materials nanostructure. 11 As a result, most of the studies focused on controlling the stacking structure of GO laminates to optimize the membrane separation performance. A  er being dispersed in water solution, GO nanosheets are assembled into laminates using methods based on mechanical external forces such as dip-coating, 12  ltration 13 and spin-coating. 14 However, it has been proven that the

As a result, it has been well demonstrated 1,4 -7 that GO-based membranes with well-assembled laminar structure can be engineered to provide nanochannels for selective molecular transport. This leads to a success application of GO-based membranes in water treatment due to the highly hydrophilic characteristic. 4 -10 Generally, separation properties of membrane can be  nely tuned by materials nanostructure. 11 As a result, most of the studies focused on controlling the stacking structure of GO laminates to optimize the membrane separation performance. texts.label: text
texts.orig: intrinsic repulsive electrostatic forces between the carboxyl groups of GO layers can cause inevitable local disordered stacking structure during GO assembly process. 2 Non-selective defects are then generated, which is negative for e ff ective separation.
texts.text: intrinsic repulsive electrostatic forces between the carboxyl groups of GO layers can cause inevitable local disordered stacking structure during GO assembly process. 2 Non-selective defects are then generated, which is negative for e ff ective separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally, the interlayer space of 2D GO nanosheets has been demonstrated to provide nanochannels for selective molecular transport. 10,15 -18 However, it still remains a great challenge to  nely regulate the 2D interlayer nanochannels for precise molecular separation of small molecules, such as water desalination and gas sieving. Being di ff erent with the methods based on mechanical external forces, in this work, we applied chemical strategy of  nely tailoring the GO-derived nanochannels by controlling their surface oxygenated functionalities (Fig. 1). Indeed, the size of GO-derived di ff usion nanochannels (interlayer space) is dominated by the amount of oxygencontaining groups on GO nanosheets. 19 Through the method of controllably removing the oxygenated groups, the GO nanochannels can be manipulated to subnanometer for highlyselective gas transport. Moreover, serving as a Lewis acid or a Lewis base, CO2 molecules can strongly interact with oxygenated groups on GO, especially hydroxyl and carboxy. 17,20 Hence, the interactions between GO nanochannels and CO2 molecules is expected to be signi  cantly in  uenced by oxygencontaining groups, which is the critical factor for tuning the CO2 adsorption behaviors in 2D GO nanochannels. Herein, this proposed bottom-up strategy focusing on improving membrane fabrication from origin, is considered to be easily controlled and scaled up, which can also be extended to other twodimensional materials membranes.
texts.text: Generally, the interlayer space of 2D GO nanosheets has been demonstrated to provide nanochannels for selective molecular transport. 10,15 -18 However, it still remains a great challenge to  nely regulate the 2D interlayer nanochannels for precise molecular separation of small molecules, such as water desalination and gas sieving. Being di ff erent with the methods based on mechanical external forces, in this work, we applied chemical strategy of  nely tailoring the GO-derived nanochannels by controlling their surface oxygenated functionalities (Fig. 1). Indeed, the size of GO-derived di ff usion nanochannels (interlayer space) is dominated by the amount of oxygencontaining groups on GO nanosheets. 19 Through the method of controllably removing the oxygenated groups, the GO nanochannels can be manipulated to subnanometer for highlyselective gas transport. Moreover, serving as a Lewis acid or a Lewis base, CO2 molecules can strongly interact with oxygenated groups on GO, especially hydroxyl and carboxy. 17,20 Hence, the interactions between GO nanochannels and CO2 molecules is expected to be signi  cantly in  uenced by oxygencontaining groups, which is the critical factor for tuning the CO2 adsorption behaviors in 2D GO nanochannels. Herein, this proposed bottom-up strategy focusing on improving membrane fabrication from origin, is considered to be easily controlled and scaled up, which can also be extended to other twodimensional materials membranes.

texts.orig: We prepared GO nanosheets by modi  ed Hummer's method. 3 Subsequently, a lot of oxygenated groups were incorporated onto GO layers, such as epoxy, hydroxy, carbonyl, and carboxy groups. These polar groups enable the stable dispersion of GO in solvent (Fig. 2a). The single or double-layered GO nanosheets were with the thickness of 1 -2 nm and lateral size of 1 -2 m m(Fig. 2b), which is proved to be suitable for embedding GO nanosheets into polymeric environment to fabricating GO -

texts.label: text
texts.orig: polymer membranes. 17 Controlling the amount of oxygenated groups can be realized by gradually reduction of original highly oxidized GO using methods of chemical reduction, intercalation or thermal annealing. 21 However, the former two methods also produce large amount of heteroatoms, which will signi  -cantly disrupt the ordered GO stacking structure as well as the interfacial properties between GO and polymer chains, thus to in  uence the dispersibility of GO in polymeric environment. Therefore, a facile thermal annealing method (see the details in ESI † ) on the basis of the analysis of the thermal properties of GO (Fig. S1 † ) was carried out to obtain reduced GO by controllably removing the oxygen-containing groups. GO powders were reduced under 180  C for 5, 10 and 24 h, which were denoted as rGO-5 h, rGO-10 h and rGO-24 h, respectively. Fig. 2c showed that oxygen-containing functionalized groups were gradually lost during the thermal reduction process. In addition, as proven by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. S2 † ), epoxy and hydroxyl groups occupy the majority of these functionalized groups on GO. A  er thermal annealing, the amount of epoxy and hydroxyl groups were sharply reduced while carbonyl and carboxy groups were not obviously removed. GO with di ff erent O/C ratios of 0.23, 0.41, 0.55 and 0.65 were  nally obtained. Thermal annealing under higher temperature (usually up to 400  C) is e ff ective to remove almost the oxygencontaining groups, forming restored p -conjugated structure, however, it can also cause severe irregular restacking of graphene sheets in polymeric environment due to the prevailing van der Waals interaction.
texts.text: polymer membranes. 17 Controlling the amount of oxygenated groups can be realized by gradually reduction of original highly oxidized GO using methods of chemical reduction, intercalation or thermal annealing. 21 However, the former two methods also produce large amount of heteroatoms, which will signi  -cantly disrupt the ordered GO stacking structure as well as the interfacial properties between GO and polymer chains, thus to in  uence the dispersibility of GO in polymeric environment. Therefore, a facile thermal annealing method (see the details in ESI † ) on the basis of the analysis of the thermal properties of GO (Fig. S1 † ) was carried out to obtain reduced GO by controllably removing the oxygen-containing groups. GO powders were reduced under 180  C for 5, 10 and 24 h, which were denoted as rGO-5 h, rGO-10 h and rGO-24 h, respectively. Fig. 2c showed that oxygen-containing functionalized groups were gradually lost during the thermal reduction process. In addition, as proven by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. S2 † ), epoxy and hydroxyl groups occupy the majority of these functionalized groups on GO. A  er thermal annealing, the amount of epoxy and hydroxyl groups were sharply reduced while carbonyl and carboxy groups were not obviously removed. GO with di ff erent O/C ratios of 0.23, 0.41, 0.55 and 0.65 were  nally obtained. Thermal annealing under higher temperature (usually up to 400  C) is e ff ective to remove almost the oxygencontaining groups, forming restored p -conjugated structure, however, it can also cause severe irregular restacking of graphene sheets in polymeric environment due to the prevailing van der Waals interaction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: GO with rich surface chemistry property can easily interact with polymer chains owing to the compatible interfacial properties. This enables the highly e ffi cient self-assembly of GO nanosheets in polymeric environment to fabricate membranes. We selected polyether block amide (PEBA) as the polymer, owing to its abundant functional groups such as -N -H -, H -N -C ] O, and O -C ] O. Then, GO with di ff erent O/C ratios of 0.23, 0.41, 0.55 and 0.65 were dispersed in mixed solvent of water and ethanol ( u ethanol : u water ¼ 70 : 30) to fabricate membranes, which can be denoted as GO-0.23, GO-0.41, GO-0.55 and GO0.65, respectively. For comparison, pure PEBA membrane was also fabricated. Digital images of dense membranes containing GOwith di ff erent O/C ratios were shown in Fig. S3. † The change of membrane color indicates the di ff erence of GO O/C ratios. It can be seen that GO at low O/C ratio caused visible local aggregation in polymer membrane. Compared with the surface scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of pure PEBA membrane in Fig. S4, † GO-0.55 membrane showed corrugated surface with some bulges on it (Fig. 2d). The as-prepared membrane with rough surface demonstrated the presence of GO laminates [see atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. S5 † ]. Side-view SEM images clearly showed bulk-like section, which can be ascribed to the presence of the stacked
texts.text: GO with rich surface chemistry property can easily interact with polymer chains owing to the compatible interfacial properties. This enables the highly e ffi cient self-assembly of GO nanosheets in polymeric environment to fabricate membranes. We selected polyether block amide (PEBA) as the polymer, owing to its abundant functional groups such as -N -H -, H -N -C ] O, and O -C ] O. Then, GO with di ff erent O/C ratios of 0.23, 0.41, 0.55 and 0.65 were dispersed in mixed solvent of water and ethanol ( u ethanol : u water ¼ 70 : 30) to fabricate membranes, which can be denoted as GO-0.23, GO-0.41, GO-0.55 and GO0.65, respectively. For comparison, pure PEBA membrane was also fabricated. Digital images of dense membranes containing GOwith di ff erent O/C ratios were shown in Fig. S3. † The change of membrane color indicates the di ff erence of GO O/C ratios. It can be seen that GO at low O/C ratio caused visible local aggregation in polymer membrane. Compared with the surface scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of pure PEBA membrane in Fig. S4, † GO-0.55 membrane showed corrugated surface with some bulges on it (Fig. 2d). The as-prepared membrane with rough surface demonstrated the presence of GO laminates [see atomic force microscope (AFM) images in Fig. S5 † ]. Side-view SEM images clearly showed bulk-like section, which can be ascribed to the presence of the stacked

texts.label: text
texts.orig: GO laminates in PEBA layer, with the size of 1 -2 m m that is in agreement of AFM characterization shown in Fig. 2b. This can be further con  rmed by TEM characterization as shown in Fig. 2e. GO nanosheets assembled into well-de  ned laminar structure with subnanometer interlayer nanochannels. By varying O/C ratios of GO, the membranes microstructures as well as physicochemical properties were quite di ff erent. GO -polymer interactions at interface are considered to be dominated by the state of oxygenated groups that provide interaction sites for interlocking GO nanosheets with polymer. Thus, GO nanosheets can assemble into well-de  ned GO laminates. As proven by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in Fig. S6, † the decomposition temperature of PEBA was obviously improved when GO was with high O/C ratio, indicating that thermal stability was enhanced owing to the strong hydrogen bonding framework. These were further demonstrated by XPS characterization (Fig. S7 † ), showing a regular shi  to higher binding energy for O 1s characteristic peaks by increasing the GO O/C ratio. This demonstrated the decreased electron cloud density of O atoms, suggesting that the hydrogen bonding frameworks can be constructed between O atoms and H atoms. 22 It is reasonable that highly interlocked GO -polymer microstructure was established by increasing the amount of oxygenated groups on GO. On the contrary, GO with low O/C ratio causes weak GO -polymer interaction, which also leads to poor dispersibility of GO nanochannels in polymeric environment. 23
texts.text: GO laminates in PEBA layer, with the size of 1 -2 m m that is in agreement of AFM characterization shown in Fig. 2b. This can be further con  rmed by TEM characterization as shown in Fig. 2e. GO nanosheets assembled into well-de  ned laminar structure with subnanometer interlayer nanochannels. By varying O/C ratios of GO, the membranes microstructures as well as physicochemical properties were quite di ff erent. GO -polymer interactions at interface are considered to be dominated by the state of oxygenated groups that provide interaction sites for interlocking GO nanosheets with polymer. Thus, GO nanosheets can assemble into well-de  ned GO laminates. As proven by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in Fig. S6, † the decomposition temperature of PEBA was obviously improved when GO was with high O/C ratio, indicating that thermal stability was enhanced owing to the strong hydrogen bonding framework. These were further demonstrated by XPS characterization (Fig. S7 † ), showing a regular shi  to higher binding energy for O 1s characteristic peaks by increasing the GO O/C ratio. This demonstrated the decreased electron cloud density of O atoms, suggesting that the hydrogen bonding frameworks can be constructed between O atoms and H atoms. 22 It is reasonable that highly interlocked GO -polymer microstructure was established by increasing the amount of oxygenated groups on GO. On the contrary, GO with low O/C ratio causes weak GO -polymer interaction, which also leads to poor dispersibility of GO nanochannels in polymeric environment. 23

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Indeed, these nanochannels are directly manipulated by the interlayer spacing of GO nanosheets. Fig. 3a exhibits the d -spacing of GO with di ff erent O/C ratios. It can be found that more oxygenated groups on GO can lead to obviously larger d -spacing. The d -spacing is considered to be composed of the thickness of single-layer carbon (0.34 nm) and the empty space between adjacent GO layers. 4,5 For GO with O/C ratios of 0.23 and 0.41, the interlayer empty space can be calculated to 0.04 and 0.27 nm, respectively, which can hardly allow any molecules to pass through. Fortunately, a  er increasing O/C ratio to 0.55, the interlayer empty space rose to approximately 0.36 nm [between the kinetic diameter of CO2 (0.33 nm) and N2 (0.364 nm)], which served as the nanochannels for molecular sieving transport. However, with the highest O/C ratio of 0.65, the interlayer empty space was enlarged to 0.5 nm, which is too large for selective di ff usion of gas molecules. The enlarged interlayer empty space is believed to increase the gas permeability.

It can be found that more oxygenated groups on GO can lead to obviously larger d -spacing. Fortunately, a  er increasing O/C ratio to 0.55, the interlayer empty space rose to approximately 0.36 nm [between the kinetic diameter of CO2 (0.33 nm) and N2 (0.364 nm)], which served as the nanochannels for molecular sieving transport. However, with the highest O/C ratio of 0.65, the interlayer empty space was enlarged to 0.5 nm, which is too large for selective di ff usion of gas molecules. The enlarged interlayer empty space is believed to increase the gas permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It has been well demonstrated that oxygenated groups on GO can signi  cantly in  uence the adsorption ability of gas molecules in GO-based membrane, leading to distinct gas permeation behaviors. As proven by the study of Li et al. , 13 GO showed the gas adsorption which followed the sequence of CO2 > CH4 > N2 > H2. Therefore, the membranes adsorption properties can be e ff ectively tailored by the amount of oxygenated groups. As shown in Fig. 3b, di ff erent membranes were tested for speci  c adsorption of CO2 and N2. CO2 adsorption capability was signi  cantly enhanced with the GO O/C ratios, while N2 was hardly adsorbed in all these membranes (Fig. S8 † ). This is because of the favorable interactions between the polar groups on GO nanosheets (such as -COOH and -OH) and CO2

As shown in Fig. 3b, di ff erent membranes were tested for speci  c adsorption of CO2 and N2. CO2 adsorption capability was signi  cantly enhanced with the GO O/C ratios, while N2 was hardly adsorbed in all these membranes (Fig. S8 † ). This is because of the favorable interactions between the polar groups on GO nanosheets (such as -COOH and -OH) and CO2

texts.orig: molecules. 14 GO-0.65 membrane exhibited the strongest CO2 adsorption ability, which was 1.64 times that of pure PEBA membrane. It can be found that GO nanosheets endowed the GO -PEBA membrane with preferential CO2 adsorption and transport, which is important for creating fast and selective nanochannels for CO2 molecules.
texts.text: molecules. 14 GO-0.65 membrane exhibited the strongest CO2 adsorption ability, which was 1.64 times that of pure PEBA membrane. It can be found that GO nanosheets endowed the GO -PEBA membrane with preferential CO2 adsorption and transport, which is important for creating fast and selective nanochannels for CO2 molecules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The as-prepared membranes were further studied by gas permeation tests with H2, CO2, N2 and CH4. Fig. 4 shows distinct gas permeation behaviors of membranes containing GO with di ff erent O/C ratios. Compared with the tortuous pathways through parallel-stacked pure GO membranes, this type of nanocomposite membrane with inclined and even vertical GO stacks provides more straight and upright pathways, exhibiting much shorter transport distance. 17 As a result, gas molecules with appropriate dynamic diameter and a ffi nity with GOcan transport fast in the assembled 2D nanochannels. It can be seen that CO2 molecules di ff use much faster than other gases, which is owing to its preferential adsorption and di ff usion properties. A dramatic growth of CO2 permeability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: occurred from GO-0.41 to GO-0.55 membrane (increased about 60%) with the increasing of GO interlayer spacing from 0.27 to 0.36 nm (Fig. 3a). In addition, the gas selectivity of CO2/N2 also increased obviously when O/C ratio of GO increased to 0.55. That is because that CO2 molecules with kinetic diameter of 0.33 nm can transport more fast and selectively in GO-derived nanochannels with interlayer height of 0.36 nm than in polymer environment. 17,20 N2 molecules with kinetic diameter of 0.364 nm can hardly travel through the con  ned space between the GO layers. Although the kinetic diameter of CH4 (0.38 nm) is even larger than that of N2, the selectivity of CO2/CH4 did not increased much. It is due to the fact that GO can also adsorb CH4 molecules. 13 Onthe basis of the solution-di ff usion theory, 24 the permeability of CH4 also increased when incorporating GO nanosheets. When O/C ratio rose to 0.65, all the tested gases showed continued increase of permeability while the gas selectivity including CO2/N2, CO2/CH4 and CO2/H2 dropped, indicating the weakening of molecular sieving e ff ect of GO nanochannels with large size (0.50 nm). In addition, membranes containing GO with low O/C ratio showed decreased selectivity compared with GO-0.55 membrane
texts.text: occurred from GO-0.41 to GO-0.55 membrane (increased about 60%) with the increasing of GO interlayer spacing from 0.27 to 0.36 nm (Fig. 3a). In addition, the gas selectivity of CO2/N2 also increased obviously when O/C ratio of GO increased to 0.55. That is because that CO2 molecules with kinetic diameter of 0.33 nm can transport more fast and selectively in GO-derived nanochannels with interlayer height of 0.36 nm than in polymer environment. 17,20 N2 molecules with kinetic diameter of 0.364 nm can hardly travel through the con  ned space between the GO layers. Although the kinetic diameter of CH4 (0.38 nm) is even larger than that of N2, the selectivity of CO2/CH4 did not increased much. It is due to the fact that GO can also adsorb CH4 molecules. 13 Onthe basis of the solution-di ff usion theory, 24 the permeability of CH4 also increased when incorporating GO nanosheets. When O/C ratio rose to 0.65, all the tested gases showed continued increase of permeability while the gas selectivity including CO2/N2, CO2/CH4 and CO2/H2 dropped, indicating the weakening of molecular sieving e ff ect of GO nanochannels with large size (0.50 nm). In addition, membranes containing GO with low O/C ratio showed decreased selectivity compared with GO-0.55 membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Fig. 4b). It is due to that gas di ff usion in as-prepared membrane was hindered when GO laminates were with a low content of oxygen-containing groups, because of the poor dispersibility and even aggregation of GO caused by the weak hydrogen bonding frameworks (Fig. S6 and S7 † ). Actually, this led to gas barrier e ff ect since the CO2 permeability of GO-0.23 was even lower than that of pure PEBA membrane. Moreover, the microdefects gradually generated during reduction process (Fig. S9 † ) also caused nonselective gas permeation, leading to decrease of gas selectivity. The comparison of gas permeation mechanisms is presented in Fig. 1 and further discussed in ESI, † which demonstrates the dominate e ff ect of GO oxygenated groups. Future research should be carried out to e ff ectively increase the amount of GO nanochannels without agglomeration, so as to obtain better separation performance.
texts.text: (Fig. 4b). It is due to that gas di ff usion in as-prepared membrane was hindered when GO laminates were with a low content of oxygen-containing groups, because of the poor dispersibility and even aggregation of GO caused by the weak hydrogen bonding frameworks (Fig. S6 and S7 † ). Actually, this led to gas barrier e ff ect since the CO2 permeability of GO-0.23 was even lower than that of pure PEBA membrane. Moreover, the microdefects gradually generated during reduction process (Fig. S9 † ) also caused nonselective gas permeation, leading to decrease of gas selectivity. The comparison of gas permeation mechanisms is presented in Fig. 1 and further discussed in ESI, † which demonstrates the dominate e ff ect of GO oxygenated groups. Future research should be carried out to e ff ectively increase the amount of GO nanochannels without agglomeration, so as to obtain better separation performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 5, compared with pure PEBA membrane, the GO-0.55 membrane showed both signi  cantly enhanced CO2 permeability and selectivity, breaking the traditional ' trade-o ff ' relationship. 25,26 Also, the GO-0.55 membrane exhibited highest separation performance of CO2/N2 (CO2 permeability: 97 barrer, CO2/N2 selectivity: 86), surpassing the upper-bound for state-of-the-art membranes, such as zeolite, 27 silica, 27 CMS, 28 pure GO, 29 PIM 30 as well as MOF-based membranes. 31 -33 It is demonstrated that the oxygen-containing groups can  nely manipulate the size of GO nanochannels and CO2 adsorption behaviors, thus, endow the as-prepared membrane with highly e ffi cient CO2 separation performance, which o ff ers promising potential for practical implementation of CO2 capture.
texts.text: As shown in Fig. 5, compared with pure PEBA membrane, the GO-0.55 membrane showed both signi  cantly enhanced CO2 permeability and selectivity, breaking the traditional ' trade-o ff ' relationship. 25,26 Also, the GO-0.55 membrane exhibited highest separation performance of CO2/N2 (CO2 permeability: 97 barrer, CO2/N2 selectivity: 86), surpassing the upper-bound for state-of-the-art membranes, such as zeolite, 27 silica, 27 CMS, 28 pure GO, 29 PIM 30 as well as MOF-based membranes. 31 -33 It is demonstrated that the oxygen-containing groups can  nely manipulate the size of GO nanochannels and CO2 adsorption behaviors, thus, endow the as-prepared membrane with highly e ffi cient CO2 separation performance, which o ff ers promising potential for practical implementation of CO2 capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, the role of the oxygenated groups of graphene oxide (GO) for elaborately tailoring GO-derived nanochannels was demonstrated in this work. We found that GO with high content of oxygen-containing groups can show better dispersibility in polymeric environment and stronger hydrogen bonding frameworks between GO and polymer chains. Moreover, the size of GO-derived nanochannels and the adsorption properties were simultaneously in  uenced by controllable removing oxygenated groups on GO. The assembled GO
texts.text: In summary, the role of the oxygenated groups of graphene oxide (GO) for elaborately tailoring GO-derived nanochannels was demonstrated in this work. We found that GO with high content of oxygen-containing groups can show better dispersibility in polymeric environment and stronger hydrogen bonding frameworks between GO and polymer chains. Moreover, the size of GO-derived nanochannels and the adsorption properties were simultaneously in  uenced by controllable removing oxygenated groups on GO. The assembled GO

texts.label: text
texts.orig: laminates with appropriate O/C ratio of 0.55 can provide subnanometer interlayer nanochannels (0.36 nm) with CO2-philic properties for preferential permeation of CO2 molecules. The as-prepared membrane exhibited excellent CO2/N2 separation performance, transcending upper-bound for state-of-the-art membranes, which is promising for CO2 capture. Moreover, this study also deepens the understanding of the molecular transport mechanisms in con  ned nanochannels and provides new insights in designing  exible and large-area 2D nanomaterials-based  lms and devices applied for nano  uidic, catalytic and related  elds.
texts.text: laminates with appropriate O/C ratio of 0.55 can provide subnanometer interlayer nanochannels (0.36 nm) with CO2-philic properties for preferential permeation of CO2 molecules. The as-prepared membrane exhibited excellent CO2/N2 separation performance, transcending upper-bound for state-of-the-art membranes, which is promising for CO2 capture. Moreover, this study also deepens the understanding of the molecular transport mechanisms in con  ned nanochannels and provides new insights in designing  exible and large-area 2D nanomaterials-based  lms and devices applied for nano  uidic, catalytic and related  elds.
